summary:
Augustus (r. 27 BC–14 AD) was the first Roman Emperor — though he carefully avoided that title, calling himself princeps ('first citizen') and presenting his rule as a restored Republic — who ended decades of civil war and established the political system that would govern the Roman world for five centuries. His reign is one of history's remarkable examples of revolutionary change accomplished through conservative presentation.

Gaius Octavius, born in 63 BC, was Julius Caesar's great-nephew who was named his heir in Caesar's will. After Caesar's [[event:1079|Assassination of Julius Caesar]], Octavian navigated the dangerous politics of the Roman world with extraordinary skill, using the [[event:1080|Second Triumvirate]] to destroy his enemies, then turning on his last ally Mark Antony. His victory at the [[event:1082|Battle of Actium]] in 31 BC left him in unchallenged control of the Roman state, its armies, and the enormous wealth of Egypt.

Octavian's genius was in recognizing that the Republic's forms had to be preserved even as its substance was abolished. He returned the provinces to Senate control in a carefully staged settlement in 27 BC, receiving the honorific title 'Augustus' from a grateful Senate. He held power through control of the army, the treasury, and key provinces containing the largest military forces, combined with vast personal wealth and an extensive network of clients and dependants. The constitutional innovations were complex and incremental, but the result was clear: one man decided war and peace, appointed governors, commanded armies, and controlled foreign policy.

Augustus surrounded his new order with ideology: the restoration of traditional Roman religion, moral legislation encouraging marriage and childbirth, magnificent public building in Rome ('I found Rome a city of brick and left it a city of marble'), patronage of the poets Virgil, Horace, and Livy who celebrated Roman greatness and his own dynasty. The [[event:1084|Pax Romana Begins]] — the long peace of the Augustan era — allowed agriculture, trade, and urban life to flourish across the Mediterranean world.

His personal life was dominated by the succession problem. Having no son, he maneuvered repeatedly to establish an heir, losing his preferred candidates (Marcellus, Gaius, Lucius Caesar) to early death before settling on his stepson Tiberius. He died in 14 AD at age seventy-five, having ruled longer than any previous Roman leader, having transformed the Mediterranean world from a war-torn set of competing territories into a unified empire at its height.

Augustus is difficult to evaluate morally: he brought order and prosperity through the complete destruction of republican governance; he used ruthless violence to seize power, then governed with relative restraint. The Roman Empire he created provided the political framework within which Christianity spread and through which classical culture survived into medieval Europe.
